title: Living in Boulder vlog 1 -  24 - 2017: Bi-weekly history test page

enrichment.summary: Kyle vlogs on January 24, 2018 about his work on a Google Earth Colosseum mapping project that was interrupted by internet-caused red-X errors, which led him to pivot and build a bi-weekly history test page for his website linking decades and years to Wikipedia articles. He also mentions staying up all night to pick up his mom from surgery and a doctor appointment about a lump he fears may be cancerous.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: plans to take his own test and expects to score poorly, which he sees as motivation to improve
openly intends to get high before taking it, framing it as a motivator

stances.topic: linking to Wikipedia vs. copying content
stances.position: prefers linking to Wikipedia because it stays dynamic and current
worried it looks like cancer; has a doctor appointment Monday

claims.subject: Colosseum
claims.claim: I was looking at the model of the Colosseum to picture in my head where the stairs were and what I'm going to make them look like.

claims.subject: Colosseum

claims.claim: All of these red Xs began to appear all over Google Earth where I was working so I couldn't do my work because they're solid red Xs.

claims.claim: The red Xs happen when my internet gets really bad, it's the way that this map works on Google Earth.

claims.subject: Google Earth
claims.claim: This map is so big, it's a map of the entire city that you can't edit at all and it's connected to the internet.

claims.subject: computer

claims.subject: computer
claims.claim: I reset the password to get the operating system update.

claims.subject: Google Earth
claims.claim: After the update, Google Earth was working again.

claims.subject: website
claims.claim: I made the numbers to decades and years chart and made the different tests for the history test.

claims.subject: Wikipedia
claims.claim: I decided to have the test questions from Wikipedia.

claims.subject: Wikipedia

claims.subject: website

claims.subject: sleep
claims.claim: I didn't get to sleep last night, I stayed up until like 4:30.

claims.subject: history test
claims.claim: The test takes an hour, no matter how many questions there are, you spend a minute on each one.

claims.claim: It takes an hour for the test, another half hour to grade it.

claims.claim: I have a doctor's appointment on Monday because there's a lump that looks like when it was cancer.

claims.subject: lump
claims.claim: The lump is sore and looks just like when it was cancer.

transcript.text: Hello. It's 3.41 p.m. on January 24th, 2018.
So, this is my vlog.
It's been just a couple of days.
So, yesterday on the 23rd, I was trying to...
I was looking at the model of the Coliseum to picture in my head.
And, you know, figure out where the stairs were.
And what I'm going to make them look like.
And I figured it out.
In fact, the stairs up above still exist.
I could see them on the little earth.
That's pretty cool.
Yeah, and so I traced these stairs to get to the second level.
2B from 2A.
And I was going to do a screen capture.
So, that I could paste it around the whole Coliseum.
Because I determined where they went.
But, at that moment, all of these red X's began to appear all over Google Earth where I was working.
So, I couldn't do my work.
Because they're solid red X's.
So, they just cover up what I'm looking at.
And that happens when...
This hasn't happened for a while.
But it happens when my internet gets really bad.
It's the way that this map works on Google Earth.
I don't have...
When I have maps on Google Earth, or usually when you put maps on Google Earth, you go to the image.
And you click on an image, and it just superimposes it.
They're on Google Earth.
But with this map, it's so big.
It's a map of the entire city.
That it's a map that you can't edit at all.
And it's connected to the internet.
So, if your internet goes bad, it turns into red X's.
So, I restarted my computer, and I opened Google Earth to see if that would help it.
And now, no Earth appeared.
So, it meant that I always had really bad internet.
So, I didn't know what to do.
I thought, maybe, what should I, like, re-download the program, you know?
And so...
But then, I had the thought, well, are there other things that I could be doing, like, on my website?
And then, I thought, oh, the bi-weekly history test page I could make now.
So, I took the opportunity to make it.
And then, I thought, I figured, as I was building it, that I could, might as well just try to fix it by getting the operating system update on my computer.
Because I haven't been getting that for a while, because you need the password.
So, I went in and reset the password.
And, um, yes, you had to type in every single time it doesn't update, and then I forget the password.
Yeah, so, uh, did the software update, and then opened up Google Earth, and it appeared.
So, Google Earth was working.
But I was already working on this page.
So, maybe pretty soon I'll do the screen capture.
By me, but not on this video.
Um, yeah, so I made the, uh, numbers to decades and years chart, and made the different tests for the history tests.
And, um, and then I thought, because I decided that I wanted to, uh, have the test, um, test from, uh, Wikipedia.
So, my first idea was to copy Wikipedia and put it onto my website, um, the page where you mouse over it, and it just appears right there.
But after I put all the breaks in, to make it, you know, make sense, it didn't work anymore.
So, I don't know, overheated or something.
So, I just decided, uh, it was actually better to just link it to Wikipedia.
Yeah, because Wikipedia is always changing, so you can keep staying dynamic that way.
Yeah, and so then I'll figure out how to, you know, grade it with the Wikipedia.
So, right now I'm, uh, in the process of, uh, linking all of the years and decades that it tested to my page, or to the chart.
And, uh, and then I'm going to write a, uh, little intro page to it.
Like, yeah, and then eventually take the test.
That'll be interesting, um, it'll be embarrassing, probably.
I'll get my ass kicked, because I'm probably not that good at history, because I'll probably get really high before I take it.
We'll see.
It'll, what it'll do is it'll motivate me, you know, to do better.
It'll motivate me a lot.
And then, yeah, I'll get really good that way.
Okay, so, um, it's 3.46 today.
I didn't get to sleep last night, so I should get to sleep pretty soon.
Uh, I stayed up, because I got up at, like, 4.30 or something yesterday.
And, uh, I had to stay up all night, because I had to pick up my mom from surgery.
She had needles stuck in her neck to help from finger pain.
Uh, the nerves.
Uh, let's see, I'll pick her up.
Then she made me a nice omelet.
Um, yeah, so, um, uh, yeah, maybe I'll do some more links.
Maybe I'll just go to sleep.
But, um, yeah, excited to do this test.
I figured you'd take, oh, so what you do is you take it for an hour.
Or, if it had, you know, if the subject, if you're doing AD, it has 200 decades.
But you, but no matter how many questions, you know, uh, random numbers you can get, um,
Uh, it's always 60 in an hour.
So, you, you do the one, you find one, and then you just spend a minute on it.
And then you go to the next one, out of 60, you sleep.
And then, uh, yeah, so I figure, you know, hour for the test, and then another half hour
To grade it, or an hour.
So, we'll see.
I got, uh, uh, uh, appointment with a doctor on Monday, because this turned into a lump that
Was, where my, it looks just like when it was cancer, in the same exact place, and it's sore.
All right, anyways, I am gonna get back to work.
Later.
And, uh, uh...
